posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I posted this on Vinous, as a follow-up to Neal Martin’s article, so please ignore if you’ve seen it there. I don’t normally post the same thing on more than one site.

Following Neal’s article, I obviously felt like trying a bottle of BAMA. It’s been a while since I last had one, probably 15 years, so I was all the more curious. After some bad bottles, I lost touch, particularly since it’s not something one comes across in shops.

The colour is as I remember from other vintages, less intense than usual Bordeaux and quite a rusty red.

On opening, the bouquet took a little coaxing before revealing notes of redcurrant, some hints of red cherry, together with tobacco and caramel, not unlike a Domaine de Chevalier 01 we had recently.

The attack is disconcerting, not in a bad way, but the taste is so different to what we have become used to that it is, literally, a conversation stopper. It’s cool, suave and deceptively light, with red berries at first, no more than a touch of cassis, before a strong hint of toffee, leading into a lifting middle section, quite salty, but where the cassis returns, then a finale which spreads to either side of the palate before a last pirouette of fading but persistent raspberry and a quintessentially dusty, lingering Margaux finish (I’m not very good at this description lark but I’ve done my best!).

We just sat there, on our terrace overlooking the river at the bottom of the garden, completely gobsmacked. Going back for more, we noticed how cool and sophisticated it tasted, how effortlessly elegant and suave. As the evening went on, the cassis and raspberry grew in persistency, without altering the perfect balance.

The wine is lovely, not the greatest Margaux ever tasted, but above all, it’s a Margaux. This is what it’s supposed to taste like! Or used to be. In the thread last week, I used Ferrière as a reference, but I was quite wrong - there is no modern equivalent, none that I know of, anyway. It of course took me back to the bottles of BAMA tasted in the past, but more so, the bottles of other wines from the 1970s and earlier: it recalled a bottle of Issan 1955, a Malescot 1974 and I think a Kirwan 1978. Some of you might not like the reference, but it reminded me too of a scene in Mondovino where Michael Broadbent, talking I think about Kirwan, says that he liked his Margaux to taste like Margaux, not Pomerol.

Bel Air Marquis could not come from anywhere else - the typicity is clear, unmistakable. Without all the oak and sugar, you can taste the stony terroir, the dusty tannins in the background are quite unique. We did actually try a modern wine with it - Citran 2010 from the night before. It’s not fair to compare them but the stylistic differences were clear - the Citran had more emphasis on the fruit and more alcohol.

The 2001 contains 12.5% alcohol. In fact, I looked at the other vintages I bought (95, 96, 00, 05, 09, 10, 11,12) - they are all 12.5%. Yes, even the 2010. Oh - so it wasn’t just “nature” which dictated that “all” 2010s should have 14% or more?? I don’t have a fixation on alcohol levels but this did surprise me. So it is winemaking after all.

It was a great experience, quite unforgettable, a wake-up call, a timely reminder that all Bordeaux does not have to taste the same and shouldn’t. Bigger is not always better.

Anyway, kudos to Robert Alfert, whose posts about the 96 and the 00 inspired me to buy some a few months ago.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Wow that’s a fantastic note, very thoughtful
Commentary. Funny how fast the BAMA sold out at my retailer following that article.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Thanks Robert - well, it was a real game changing moment for me. One gets used to received wisdom about what wines are supposed to taste like, even without going to the extremes of the spoofed up stuff, because as you know, there aren’t any wines anymore which taste like Bel Air Marquis. It did make me think about the experiment with Le Puy, except that BAMA is IMHO a whole lot better.

BTW, Neal’s article was not the first by a wine publication - I discovered that Jancis Robinson had also written something last October:

jancisrobinson.com – 10 Oct 17

BAMA – the oak-free Margaux

BAMA – the oak-free Margaux

I’m sorry your shop’s run out! Well, plenty more to come, since he has apparently got 100 000 bottles in his cellar! Over here, the article has had the opposite effect - my retailer now has a whole swathe of old vintages, going back to the 1970:

vins-etonnants.com

Chateau Bel Air Marquis d'Aligre - vin de margaux vins-etonnants

Bel Air Marquis d'Aligre , margaux, boyer

For once, I’d be more than happy if more people did buy the wine, whatever the consequences regarding the prices: if it contributes to a general realization that we have been told a lot of balderdash since 1982, so much the better. Maybe winemakers will have a rethink.

link_counts.title: Chateau Bel Air Marquis d'Aligre - vin de margaux vins-etonnants

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
A very nice appreciation of this wine.

I’m afraid that it’s too late for any return to this kind of Bordeaux. The capital invested in old vines, long élevage and late release is considerable. Plus the interested constituency is small. Without intending to sound unduly pessimistic or nostalgic, the culture that created wines such Bel Air Marquis d’Aligre no longer exists.

As far as precedence goes, I wrote about BAMdA in Noble Rot last February, and as it happens served Neal the 1970 he mentions in his article! Since I only owned two bottles and the second was corked, I’m glad I picked the good bottle, as his piece truly does justice to the estate.

But the French wine press have been writing about BAMdA for quite a while now, so it has been hiding in plain sight.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Thank you for those kind words, William - and for setting the record straight about precedence. Actually, I knew that you were responsible for the 1970 - and Neal readily recognized the fact when I asked him on Vinous! I don’t know Noble Rot, so obviously I missed that one. In France, I only occasionally buy La Revue des Vins de France, so if they did publish something, I missed it too! I do know that various bloggers have written about it though, since I have been reading them over the past few days. It’s always been a hit for French wine geeks, who introduced me to the wine in the first place.

I must say I’m tempted by the 1970 which is offered over here, but June is not the best time for shipping a 37 year old wine, so perhaps next winter.

Sadly, I suspect you’re right about the future for this type of wine. I only know of one château which does the same thing, keeping very old vintages and run by a similarly idiosyncratic owner: Le Tréhon in Bégadan, but I wasn’t convinced by all the wines I tried. All the more reason to get a few more Bel Airs!

Tonight, we opened a 2000 to compare. Quite different. I shall write my impressions tomorrow, but the first sips are very promising.

Cheers!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Julian Marshall:

Thank you for those kind words, William - and for setting the record straight about precedence. Actually, I knew that you were responsible for the 1970 - and Neal readily recognized the fact when I asked him on Vinous! I don’t know Noble Rot, so obviously I missed that one. In France, I only occasionally buy La Revue des Vins de France, so if they did publish something, I missed it too! I do know that various bloggers have written about it though, since I have been reading them over the past few days. It’s always been a hit for French wine geeks, who introduced me to the wine in the first place.

I must say I’m tempted by the 1970 which is offered over here, but June is not the best time for shipping a 37 year old wine, so perhaps next winter.

Sadly, I suspect you’re right about the future for this type of wine. I only know of one château which does the same thing, keeping very old vintages and run by a similarly idiosyncratic owner: Le Tréhon in Bégadan, but I wasn’t convinced by all the wines I tried. All the more reason to get a few more Bel Airs!

Tonight, we opened a 2000 to compare. Quite different. I shall write my impressions tomorrow, but the first sips are very promising.

Cheers!

The 1970 is definitely worth a shot. I double decanted it and regretted it for once, as it was more extravert and fruity on opening. More elegant and melted than even 85, for example, but very pretty.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Thanks, William - the 70 is on my shopping list!

BTW - since you’re a Burgundy specialist - in her article, Jancis R says that Bel Air Marquis wines are “almost burgundian in their build and lusciousness”. I know little about Burgundy but I don’t get that at all - what do you think?

Just a few impressions about the 2000:

Comparing them side by side, the first big difference is the colour. The 01 is much more evolved than the 00, which has no rustiness whatsoever.

The bouquet of the 00 is less Graves-like, with raspberry and violet dominating the red berries. There are notes of tobacco, but these are less prominent and no hint of caramel at all.

The attack is fresh, vibrant and a lot more youthful, with a little more cassis than the 01 and again, no hint of toffee. Over the course of the evening, the middle section developed notes of plum, with hints of rose-hip syrup on the effortlessly classy, dusty finish. Much fruitier and sweeter, still very Margalais, but less of a culture shock than the 01.

The 2000 is clearly the better wine and the one to buy for the future. Tasted with the 01, one can see that it’s not ready yet. It hasn’t quite the same complexity or the same originality. The fruit is still a bit primary and it masks the terroir. In another five years it’ll be stunning, but for now the 01 is the more compelling.

Interestingly, the 00 does taste like Ferrière, so I will contradict myself yet again!

I’m glad I started with the 2001 but I’ll buy more 2000.

posts.name: Kelly Flynn
posts.cooked: By the way…Chambers Street Wines NYC sent out an email yesterday advertising the BAM 1970, 1995, 1996, 1998, 2000, 2004, 2009 and 2010. I think wine arrives late Summer.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Just as a footnote, I tried the rest of the 00 against a Giscours 2000 last night. The latter is an excellent example of Giscours, probably the best I’ve ever had, so a benchmark for Margaux in 2000.

The Bel Air had had another two days in the fridge and was clearly better for them, which would suggest that it needs extended aeration next time out. The fruit had softened and the finish was much longer. The link to the 01 was a lot clearer.

Tasted side by side with the Giscours, the Bel Air wasn’t overwhelmed, on the contrary even: the Giscours is deeper and more masculine, with a lot more cassis and structure, but the Bel Air’s charm and persistence shone through. I would say that the Giscours will be the better wine in the future, but alongside the Bel Air it tasted quite tannic, with a much shorter finish. The Bel Air’s finish lasted a good 20 seconds longer than the Giscours’s.

So confirmation that Bel Air Marquis d’Aligre does indeed produce CC standard wines. If I had to choose between the two, I’m not sure which I would prefer - but the Bel Air is half the price of the Giscours.
